retrieved_docs.title: Tinnitus -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: Twenty percent of persons visiting tinnitus clinics have normal hearing. Some have somatosensory tinnitus. Here, stimulation from cervical or TMJD has activated the dorsal cochlear nucleus and sends impulses to the auditory center. Evidence for this is that stimulation similar to whiplash or TMJD has been shown to cause anatomical changes in the dorsal cochlear nucleus. [5] [6] [7] A rare cause of tinnitus is the Chiari malformation. Tinnitus occurs when low-lying cerebellar tonsils cause auditory nerve tension and can be unilateral or bilateral.

retrieved_docs.title: Tinnitus -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: Sleep improvement: Tinnitus can affect normal sleep, and therapy should be directed toward better sleep hygiene.

retrieved_docs.title: Tinnitus -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: The American Academy of Otolaryngology has issued clinical practice guidelines for tinnitus. These include: Stress Reduction: This includes using biofeedback, measured breathing, etc. Although stress itself is not a cause of tinnitus, as in any condition, stress and anxiety can make the condition worse. Cognitive Therapy: The more the patient understands what tinnitus is and is not, the less negative the effect is. The symptoms are reduced once the patient fully recognizes and understands that tinnitus is similar to itching. Masking: When the body hears the same sound from the cell phone or sound device, this reduces the symptoms.  There are various forms of masking. Essentially, these masking sounds take the attention away from the internal tinnitus sound and replace it with relaxing sounds. Introduction of the same sound Introduction of an altered sound Music with the tinnitus sound removed White noise or pleasant sounds

retrieved_docs.title: Pulsatile Tinnitus -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Pulsatile tinnitus is a relatively rare type of tinnitus with a complex differential diagnosis. Practitioners need to know the proper workup for such patients to identify potential conditions that can increase patient mortality and morbidity. This activity outlines the evaluation and management of pulsatile tinnitus and reviews the role of the interprofessional team in improving care for patients with this symptom.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 3 -- Community 1494
retrieved_docs.content: Meclizine hydrochloride is recognized for its therapeutic role in managing vestibular symptoms, as it may treat dizziness (الدوار). In addition to its anti‑vertigo effects, meclizine hydrochloride may also prevent vomiting (تَقَيُّء), making it useful in the prophylaxis of nausea and emesis associated with motion sickness or other vestibular disturbances. This dual action supports its use in patient care where both dizziness and the risk of vomiting need to be addressed.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 30 -- Level 3 -- Community 2263
retrieved_docs.content: Polyhydramnios, acquired immunodeficiency syndrome (AIDS), cardiac and circulatory congenital anomalies, peptic ulcer disease, and abdominal pain require thorough etiological assessment to guide the selection of appropriate medical therapy, determine the indication for surgical intervention, and design postoperative rehabilitation programs that optimize functional recovery. Abdominal pain is specifically associated with malignant hypertension, underscoring the need to evaluate severe hypertension as a potential source of visceral pain. A broad spectrum of serious conditions—including septicemia, hypersexuality, ureteral obstruction, fungal infection, malignant hypertension, congenital nervous system anomalies, tongue disorder, threatened abortion, and subdural or subarachnoid hemorrhage—serve both as etiological factors for diverse clinical presentations and as indications for surgery, rehabilitation, and targeted therapeutic strategies. Disorders presenting with dizziness or vertigo similarly demand precise identification of the underlying cause, which informs the choice of rehabilitative and therapeutic measures. The concept of an “allowed qualifier” functions as a generic etiological descriptor that can be applied to subarachnoid hemorrhage, subdural hemorrhage, and disorders linked to dizziness/vertigo and congenital nervous system anomalies, integrating etiological insight with the selection of appropriate therapeutic options and rehabilitation plans across this heterogeneous disease spectrum.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 30 -- Level 5 -- Community 2922
retrieved_docs.content: The allowed qualifier is applied to therapeutic interventions (terapie) for cardiac and circulatory congenital anomalies, for conditions associated with dizziness or vertigo, and for nervous system congenital anomalies. In each of these contexts, the use of therapy must take into account that therapy can be associated with a threat of miscarriage (تهديد بالإجهاض).
